entry.title: The Board Decides, the CIO Delivers: A Digital Health Platform Powered by SAP
entry.content:
In my previous posts I described SAP’s global healthcare industry strategy, a modular, data-driven, open, and interoperable platform, and I argued that a digital health platform (DHP) is ultimately a business decision that belongs on the CEO’s desk. Once the board has made that call, however, the mandate lies with the CIO, who turns a strategic ambition into a governed and sequenced delivery program that runs alongside daily operations without disrupting them. This post is written for that very role, and it tries to focus on two questions: what does a digital health platform powered by SAP actually consist of, and why does it make the CIO’s task easier instead of adding yet another program to an already full portfolio?

A landscape grown by change, not by design

In my experience, most healthcare providers definitely do not suffer from a lack of applications; they suffer from a system landscape that has grown over many years and rarely by design, and the figures show this: a symplr survey of health system clinicians and CIOs found that a majority of organizations run somewhere between 50 and 500, sometimes even more, different software solutions to operate their healthcare business, and in the United States alone the sector saw more than 900 hospital merger and acquisition transactions between 2014 and 2024, each of which brought their own systems, teams, and technical debt into somebody else’s landscape. So, there is an electronic medical record (EMR) system, or several of them after mergers, one or more revenue cycle management (RCM) systems for billing and patient accounting, a sometimes aging enterprise resource planning (ERP) system, a number of departmental specialty systems, research databases, and a long tail of point solutions, each of which brings its own data model, its own integration approach, and its own vendor release schedule. In such a landscape, every new capability becomes its own project, and every project has to solve integration, data quality, and compliance all over again, and the pace of change of the whole institution is effectively set by whichever vendor moves slowest.

The timing pressure is real, too. Many hospitals still run SAP Business Suite 7 (ECC), whose mainstream maintenance ends in 2027, with extended maintenance through 2030 and a Transition Option in SAP Private Cloud through 2033, and in Europe the secondary-use provisions of the European Health Data Space (EHDS) Regulation begin to apply in March 2029, which means the CIO’s delivery window for both obligations is essentially the same three to four years. And the platform market itself is being shaped in exactly that window: Gartner estimated in its April 2024 research "Innovation Insight: Digital Health Platforms Accelerate Transformation" that the digital health platform market was only around five percent penetrated, with adoption expected to play out over five to ten years, so the institutions making their architectural choices now are the ones whose requirements the market will form around.

The EHR-first instinct, and where it ends

Before laying out our answer, let me name the objection I hear most often, because it deserves a serious analysis. Many health systems reasonably prefer to look at their EMR vendor first for any new capability; successive Bain and KLAS surveys have found that roughly two out of three look first to their existing vendors, and especially their EMR vendor, before considering anyone else. I understand the instinct, since the EMR carries the clinical workflows and much of the clinical data, and nobody wants another vendor relationship without good reason. Yet the same market research paints a sobering picture of how that instinct works out in practice, with only about a quarter of executives in a Sage Growth survey saying their EMR vendor fully lives up to its promises. The deeper issue, though, is not vendor performance but scope: the decisions a CIO is now asked to support, from cost transparency per procedure to workforce planning to supply chain resilience to research data provisioning, depend on finance, procurement, workforce, and logistics data that does not live in the EMR and never will, and a system built to document and bill for care was simply never designed to be the enterprise data and AI foundation of a health system. This is, incidentally, also where the analyst community has landed: Gartner has addressed its digital health platform research explicitly to healthcare provider CIOs since its initial Market Guide in 2021, describing a shift away from monolithic, EMR-centric application suites towards a platform architecture, and in its most recent framing "Digital Health Platforms (Transitioning to Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms) Reviews and Ratings" positions the category as evolving into Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms whose purpose is to let a CIO adjust the application portfolio at the pace of business change. That last phrase describes the job better than I ever could.

Connect, govern, innovate, transform

Our strategy for the digital health platform powered by SAP addresses exactly this in four dimensions: connect, govern, innovate, and transform. Let me take them in the order an actual delivery program would.

To connect means to bring disparate systems into a single, coherent view across clinical and enterprise operations. The platform speaks the standards your landscape already uses, among them HL7 FHIR®, openEHR, IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise), OMOP (the common data model of the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership), and, in Germany, ISiK as the national interoperability standard for hospital information systems. It connects EMR and revenue cycle management systems as well as EHDS-related sources, alongside SAP and non-SAP applications, whether they run as software as a service, cloud-native, hybrid, or on-premises. The AnyEMR/MultiEMR and AnyRCM/MultiRCM principles I described in the strategy post apply here, too: the platform integrates across whatever clinical and billing systems you have chosen rather than locking you into a single vendor’s stack, and since SAP does not build EMR or RCM systems itself, we unify across them together with and through our partner ecosystem.

To govern is, for the CIO, the most consequential of the four: in most institutions today, governance is a per-project exercise: every integration project defines its own access rules, every research request triggers its own anonymization effort, every audit finding produces its own remediation plan, and the sum of all this is a patchwork of controls that nobody can fully describe, let alone defend under time pressure. On the platform, governance becomes a managed capability instead. Master data governance, identity and access, consent and rights management, pseudonymization and anonymization, and data sovereignty compliance are built into the architecture and travel with every data product, so a rule defined once, for example who may see identifiable patient data and under which consent, is enforced wherever that data flows, whether into an operational dashboard, an AI model, or a research extract. That does not make audit readiness automatic, because audits will always also examine processes and people, but it makes audit readiness sustainable, since the technical controls no longer have to be reconstructed and re-documented for every new use of the data. And where residency requirements demand it, the same architecture can be deployed on sovereign-cloud options, so that the jurisdiction question is answered by design rather than negotiated per project. This is also where regulation stops being only a burden: an institution whose consent, provenance, and de-identification are managed capabilities enters the EHDS era prepared, while one that handles them project by project will spend those same years catching up.

To innovate then builds on that foundation rather than around it: a composition layer with analytics from descriptive to predictive, knowledge graphs for reasoning across clinical, operational, and financial domains, healthcare-specific semantics and ontologies, which are expanding to include terminologies such as SNOMED CT and the OMOP data models, and AI domain models including clinical natural language processing, all accessible through build tools from low-code to pro-code, so that extensions live safely outside a clean core and survive the next upgrade, a lesson many of us learned the hard way in the ERP world. And to transform is simply what follows when the first three are in place: decisions powered by governed data and embedded AI, at a pace the institution sets for itself.

The architecture, seen from the delivery side

At the top level, the DHP architecture consists of three layers, and increasingly a fourth. A data foundation provides clinical, enterprise, and ecosystem connectivity, including the healthcare interoperability standards mentioned above. A digital innovation layer carries the intelligence and the health-specific business context. A healthcare experiences layer offers applications such as patient portals, clinical workplaces, health apps and dashboards, and scenarios like clinical trial matching, patient identity resolution, and the EHDS health record. And on top of these sits the AI layer, in which embedded intelligence, assistants, and increasingly agentic capabilities operate on the governed layers beneath them rather than beside them. Readers who know Gartner’s published DHP reference architecture from their "Market Guide for Digital Health Platforms" will recognize the shape of the first three: an integrated data fabric with health data connectors, data curation and enrichment, metadata management, a healthcare-specific data model, and privacy and consent controls; a layer of packaged business capabilities; and a composition layer with low-code tooling for building tailored experiences. Our architecture is a concrete instantiation of exactly that pattern, and it deliberately extends it in the one dimension that has moved fastest since the pattern was first described: the AI layer on top. And for readers who want to explore how this platform category is developing across the wider market, Vince Kuraitis’s series “Platforming Healthcare – The Long View” offers a thorough long-term perspective.

What binds these layers together is the data product, and it comes in two kinds. Primary data products are sourced directly from a clinical or enterprise application and represent the original, unprocessed data: claims records from the revenue cycle management system, encounter summaries from the EMR, patient records from a clinical data repository (CDR), or financials from SAP Cloud ERP. Derived data products are curated and enriched on top of them, and here, health-specific content such as Patient 360, treatment cost, clinical workforce readiness, and patient risk scores, which deliver ready-to-use insight without additional integration effort, are being built out. Anyone who has run a hospital integration program knows why this matters: instead of maintaining n×m point-to-point integrations between sources and consumers, you build governed, reusable assets once and consume them everywhere, with lineage and policy enforced from the start.

This is what that AI layer changes in practice: because it is grounded in exactly this governed, contextualized data, and because it acts through the platform’s managed capabilities rather than around them, AI can be deployed where it demonstrably relieves pressure in daily operations. SAP Joule, our AI assistant, is being extended by SAP and partners with healthcare scenarios to address the everyday pain points of running a hospital, from clinical documentation, coding and DRG optimization, and denial management to medication replenishment, patient scheduling, regulatory reporting, and workforce planning, while differentiating industry scenarios around Patient 360, clinical workforce optimization, and denial prevention and revenue recovery build on the very same foundation. The CIO needs neither a separate AI stack nor a separate governance regime for it, and no separate integration program to feed it, because the platform itself is the AI foundation. It is also the path to what we at SAP call the Autonomous Enterprise: operations in which routine work increasingly runs itself, under the institution's own rules and controls. That kind of autonomy cannot be installed; it becomes possible only once connected data, business context, and governance are already in place, which is exactly what the layers beneath the AI provide.

That this approach works in practice is not a hypothesis: Hirslanden, Switzerland’s largest private hospital group, modernized its core processes and systems to support new clinical practices, regulatory standards, and cost reimbursement using SAP Signavio business transformation solutions, an example of how process intelligence grounded in a common platform accelerates transformation in healthcare rather than adding to the project load.

An ecosystem by design

No single vendor covers healthcare end to end, and we at SAP do not pretend to. Partners extend the platform with the clinical capabilities and industry solutions, built on SAP Business Technology Platform and SAP Business Data Cloud, delivered through SAP PartnerEdge, and available via SAP Store. For the CIO, that translates into freedom of choice at the edges and a stable, standards-based core in the middle.

A pragmatic sequence, not a big bang

Given the maintenance and regulatory horizons described above, a deliberate sequence is in fact the lower-risk path. It begins with mapping the landscape and agreeing on three to five cross-functional use cases that span clinical and operational domains. It continues with setting up the data foundation and publishing the first data products with governance in place, and with modernizing the ERP core at the institution’s own pace, be it with GROW with SAP for first-time adopters or with RISE with SAP for existing landscapes, where most providers favor separating the ERP and hospital information system transformations, with ERP first, to harmonize master data before clinical systems are subsequently integrated. It then connects the EMR, RCM, and priority partner solutions through standards-based integration including HL7 FHIR®, and it proves value early with time-boxed pilots before scaling responsibly.

The outcome is what the board asked for in the first place, and it is what running best means in healthcare: an institution that adopts innovation at its own speed, meets its regulatory obligations by design, and, where it chooses to, takes part in emerging health data collaborations on its own terms. I will not promise any CIO that a platform shrinks the portfolio overnight, because none does, and every practitioner knows that platforms are additive in the first place before they are consolidating. What it does provide is something more important: an operational model with a credible, governed path from a landscape that dictates its own terms to one that follows yours.

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review
entry.content:
In my Day 1 recap, I talked about the gap between AI investment and AI value, and closed with the ambition that the organizations that lead won't just automate what exists, they will reinvent it. Day 2 was where that ambition got concrete, showing what reinvention looks like in Finance, Customer Experience, and Supply Chain, and giving consultants a practical session on getting SAP Joule for Consultants running on live projects.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

AI is everywhere. Results aren't.

That was the opening line of the Day 2 Morning Briefing (recording), and it set the tone for everything that followed. 74% of organizations are stuck in pilot and the reason is structural: generic AI lacks business context, point solutions create patchwork, and building without governance slows everything down. Most enterprises are still assembling AI function by function, a copilot here, a point solution there. Each adds intelligence to a silo. None of them talk to each other.

SAP's answer is one system. The SAP Autonomous Suite is organized around five domains, Finance, Spend, Supply Chain, HR, and Customer Experience, where people direct, Assistants coordinate, and Agents execute. Domains move as one: a decision in Finance shows up in Supply Chain; a change in demand triggers a response in Procurement. Industry AI extends each domain with vertical knowledge and regulatory logic from day one. Explore all Joule Assistants here.

On Day 1, we looked at Joule Work as the unified workspace where this all comes together. Day 2 went deeper, showing what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Finance, CX, and Supply Chain specifically. The sessions that followed answered exactly that. Rewatch them:

  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Finance: See how a billing process map changes when agents take over the work that shouldn't need a person (recording).
  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Supply Chain Management: Get a sneak peek of the Asset and Service Assistant (recording).
  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Customer Experience: See how CX becomes a revenue function by closing the disconnect between engagement data and business outcomes (recording).

Seeing what the Autonomous Enterprise looks like in each domain is the first step. Getting there is the next. The day's final session was built for the consultants and partners doing that work. Two features released at SAP Sapphire 2026 were the headline of this session, and both are already available:

Custom Knowledge Grounding connects SAP Joule for Consultants to your organization's own content such as project files, internal knowledge repositories, delivery standards, and policies indexed via SAP AI Core Grounding. The result: answers that draw on SAP's full knowledge base and your firm's institutional knowledge at the same time. Less time searching. Responses that reflect how your organization actually works.

  • SAP Joule for Consultants Custom Knowledge Grounding
  • Enhance SAP Joule for Consultants with Custom Knowledge Grounding

Expert Workspace enables persistent personalization in SAP Joule for Consultants by introducing reusable Experts with custom instructions and file-based grounding. It is a centralized environment within the SAP Joule for Consultants console where users can create, configure, and manage Experts at design time. Experts can then be explicitly activated during conversations at run time.

  • SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace
  • Getting Started with SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace

Rewatch From Activation to Application: Getting Started with SAP Joule for Consultants.

Before I wrap up, I wanted to address the questions that came up most:

  • What does it actually cost?
    SAP Joule for Consultants is a premium AI feature.     Pricing is user-based, reflecting scenarios where value is directly tied to individual productivity. It is offered as a Per User Per Month package, with a fixed number of 35 AI Units per user. Consumption is tracked in SAP for Me. Find out more in the Whitepaper Understanding the SAP Business AI Commercial Model.
  • What are the prerequisites for activating SAP Joule for Consultants?
    As a customer or partner, you will need to have AI Units and a SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Global Account. There are no other technical dependencies and no system requirements. SAP Joule for Consultants is a standalone web-based assistant, which means on-premise, ECC, and private cloud customers can activate and use it just as easily as public cloud customers. Find out how to enable SAP Joule for Consultants in the Discovery Center Mission.

Day 1 set the foundation. Day 2 put it into practice. Continue with the full series:

  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review

entry.title: Building Custom AI Agents with SAP Joule Studio: A Practical Guide Part-1
entry.content:
Introduction

Joule is already available in SAP Launchpad for many users, and it is great for broad questions.
My challenge started when business users asked for operational actions, not guidance.

A user asked:
“Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001 and remove the delivery block for order 487.”

I am unable to directly retrieve or display information on open sales orders due to system limitations. However, I can guide you on how to use the 'Sales Order - Query' to access this information manually. Would you like me to provide instructions on how to do this?

Out of the box, Joule gave a helpful explanation, but it did not execute the business action.
So I built a custom agent in Joule Studio that could read and update sales orders in SAP S/4HANA through OData APIs.

The Two Building Blocks: Skills & Agents

Everything in Joule Studio revolves around two core concepts:

1. Skill — A Specific Task

A  skill is a single, focused capability. Think of it as one action your agent can perform. It could be:

  1. A CRUD operation (Create, Read, Update, Delete)
  2. A process or automation
  3. A  condition check
  4. Launching another Joule skill
  5. Each skill does one thing well. For example:
    • Read all sales orders
    • Update delivery block on a sales order
    • Create a new sales order
    • Delete sales order.
2. Agent — The Orchestrator

An agent is the brain that holds multiple skills together. It decides when to use which skill based on what the user asks. An agent can even call other agents — making it possible to build complex, multi-step workflows.

Anatomy of an Agent
When you create an agent in Joule Studio, you'll work with several important configuration areas:

Expertise & Instructions

  1. Expertise — Tell the agent what domain it specializes in. Is it for HR? Sales? Procurement? This helps the agent understand its boundaries.
  2. Instructions — This is where the magic happens. You're essentially writing a playbook for the agent:
    1. What to do and what not to do
    2. How to format responses
    3. When to trigger which skill
    4. Rules and guardrail

Additional Context — Common abbreviations, company-specific terminology, or any contextual information the agent might need to understand your business language.

Model Settings
You get to choose your LLM provider:

  • Anthropic
  • OpenAI
  • Google
  • MistralAI

Enable Backup LLM Provider. This sets up automatic failover — if your primary provider is slow or unavailable, the backup kicks in automatically, keeping your agent running without interruption.

MCP Servers
Add MCP (Model Context Protocol) Servers to give your agent access to additional tools and external capabilities.

Tools
Add tools that provide the agent with additional context, system data access, or the ability to perform actions.

  • calculator
  • joule skill
  • joule agent
  • Documents(Document Grounding)

Agent Output

Define how the output should be formatted:

  1. Markdown
  2. Text
  3. Data Type
  4. JSON
What I Built (Actual Implementation)

I implemented a Sales Order Assistant agent for a finance and sales operations scenario.

Scope of the agent:

  • Read sales orders with filters (customer, status, billing date, block flags)
  • Update delivery block on a specific sales order
  • Return a table-style summary for business users
  • Refuse to answer if live data cannot be read

Technical stack:

  • Joule Studio agent
  • 3 custom skills (GET, PATCH, POST)
  • SAP S/4HANA OData service: API_SALES_ORDER_SRV
  • Destination-based connectivity in test environment
  • Primary + backup LLM provider enabled
Step 1: Skills I Created

I separated skills by operation so tool selection remained predictable.

1. Read Sales Orders (GET)
Purpose: Retrieve sales orders and basic status fields.

2. Modify Sales Order (PATCH)
Purpose: Update delivery block and selected editable fields.

3. Create Sales Order (POST)
Purpose: Create a new order for approved scenarios.

Why this helped:
When I initially combined read and update into one “do everything” skill, the agent often chose the wrong operation. Splitting skills reduced tool-calling errors significantly.

Step 2: Agent Instructions I Actually Used

The single biggest improvement came from strict instructions.

Key rules I configured:

1. Always call Read Sales Orders before any status answer.
2. Call Modify Sales Order only when user intent is clearly an update.
3. Never generate order numbers, dates, or statuses without API output.
4. On 4XX/5XX, return a clear business-safe error message and next step.

Response format:

  • Always output a compact table for list queries.
  • Always include order ID, customer, billing date, and status.
  • Flag risky records: overdue billing, missing assignment, missing reference.

What changed after this:
Before strict rules, I saw occasional “plausible” but unverified responses. After adding mandatory tool-use rules, those stopped.

Step 3: Real Test Cases and Results

I tested using business-like prompts.

Test prompts:

  • “Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001.”
  • “Remove delivery block on order 500001234.”
  • “List overdue orders with missing assignment.”

Observed behavior:

  • Read queries: agent called GET skill, then summarized correctly.
  • Update queries: agent called PATCH skill with target order.
  • Failure case (invalid order): agent returned API error context and suggested verification steps.

One practical lesson:
If the API payload is too large, response quality drops. I reduced returned fields to only what users need and got more stable answers.

These were the most useful implementation learnings :

1. Keep skills narrow and explicit
Broad “multi-action” skills increase wrong tool selection.

2. Put tool-calling rules at the top of instructions
Priority placement improved consistency.

3. Add business-safe refusal behavior
If data retrieval fails, the agent must refuse to guess.

4. Minimize API response payload early
Too many fields create noisy reasoning and weaker summaries.

5. Add backup LLM provider, but validate output style
Failover worked, but I had to align formatting instructions across models.

6. Test with ambiguous prompts
Examples like “fix this order” forced me to add clarification logic before updates.

Execution Trace: What Happens Under the Hood (From My Test Runs)
For a prompt like “Show blocked orders for customer 17100001”:

1. Agent starts and loads instructions/context
2. Intent classified as data retrieval
3. GET skill called with filters
4. Raw response compressed
5. Agent applies formatting + risk flags
6. Final response returned as table

For an update prompt:

1. Agent detects action intent
2. Validates target order context via read
3. PATCH skill executes update
4. Agent confirms changed field and status

Real-World Example: Sales Order Assistant
Let me walk you through a practical example. My requirement was to build an agent that can:

  1. Handle natural language queries like *"Show all open orders for customer X"* or *"Remove delivery block on order Y"*
  2. Provide contextual insights (customer credit status, expected delivery dates)
  3. Suggest corrective actions and guide users through SAP Fiori apps

Step 1: Pick Your API

I went to the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and chose the standard OData service: `API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/`

Step 2: Create Skills

I built three separate skills — one for each operation:

Skill Operation Purpose
Read Sales OrdersGET Fetch all sales orders
Update Sales OrderPATCH Modify fields like delivery block
Create Sales OrderPOSTCreate a new sales order


The GET skill is that it retrieves all sales orders, but the agent processes the response based on your question. Ask for a specific customer? It filters and gives you only that data — not the entire list.

Step 3: Configure the Agent

Here's an example of how I set up the expertise and instructions:

Expertise:

You are an SAP Sales Order Assistant. Your expertise includes:
- Retrieving sales order details from SAP S/4HANA using OData services
- Explaining order status, delivery dates, and pricing information
- Helping users understand delivery blocks and billing workflows

Instructions:
CRITICAL RULES:
1. You MUST ALWAYS call the "Read Sales Order" skill before answering
ANY question about sales orders. NEVER answer from your own knowledge.
2. You MUST call "Modify Sales Order" skill when user tries to modify
a sales order.
3. If you cannot call the tool, inform the user that you need to
retrieve the data and cannot answer without it.

RESPONSIBILITIES:
- Retrieve sales order headers (order number, customer, billing date, status)
- Summarize order volumes by customer group, date range, or status
- Identify orders requiring attention (overdue billing, missing assignments)
- Present results in a clear table format

RESPONSE FORMAT:
| # | Sales Order | Customer Group | Billing Date | Status |
|---|------------|----------------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 4500001234 | Group 1 | 2026-05-25 | Open |

DO NOT:
- Guess or fabricate order data
- Ignore Error 4XX responses — always inform the user

 

This is a basic example — in a real scenario, you'd provide far more detailed instructions based on your specific business requirements.

Additional Context

FIELD DEFINITIONS:
- Reference → Order reference / external document number
- Customer Group 1-4 → Classification groupings for the customer
- Assignment → Cost assignment or project reference
- Billing Date → Planned billing/invoicing date
- Exchng. Rate Accntg. → Exchange rate used for accounting
- Addit. Value Date → Additional value date for payment terms

SORTING & PRIORITY RULES:
- Default sort: Billing Date (earliest first)
- Highlight orders where Billing Date < today (overdue)
- Flag orders with empty Assignment or Reference fields

ERROR HANDLING:
- If API returns Error 4XX → inform user, suggest checking
authorization or filters

ORDER STATUS LOGIC:
- Billing Date < today + no invoice = "Overdue"
- Assignment field empty = "Needs Attention"
- Reference field empty = "Missing Reference"

Step 4: Connect Skills to Agent

The connection is straightforward:

Agent → Tools → Add Skill → Select your skill

Do this for each skill. Then, in your instructions, specify when the agent should trigger which skill.

Step 5: Test

Click Test, select your destination for the action calls, select your private environment, and start chatting:

  1. Give me sales order 987
  2. Show me all blocked sales orders
  3. Remove the delivery block on order 456

What Happens Under the Hood?

When you test your agent, you can see the internal execution trace. Here's what happens when a user asks something:

User Message

 

Breaking It Down:

PhaseWhat It Does
StartInitializes the agent, loads context, expertise, and instructions
Direct PromptingChecks if the request can be answered without complex reasoning
Decomposition Breaks the request into smaller sub-tasks
Plan Creates an execution plan — decides order of steps, which APIs to call
Agent Thinking The LLM reasons about the plan and decides the action to take
ReadExecutes the API call to S/4HANA
CompressorSummarizes the raw API response to stay within token limits
Agent Thinking (2nd)Processes compressed data and formulates the answer
Final Answer

Produces the user-facing response

Refinement

Quality check for accuracy, formatting, and completeness

End

Execution completes

Conclusion

The future of enterprise AI isn't just about having an AI assistant. It's about having your AI assistant, built for your business.

Joule gives strong baseline assistance, but business operations need domain-specific actions.
Joule Studio is where that becomes practical: you define the tools, the guardrails, and the behavior.

My key takeaway after implementation:
Good agent quality is less about which model and more about:

  • strict tool-calling instructions,
  • small and clean payloads,
  • and real-world test prompts with edge cases.

If you are building your first Joule Studio agent, start with one business process, 2-3 narrow skills, and strict non-hallucination rules. You will get better results faster than trying to build a broad agent on day one.

Remember: you're essentially teaching a new team member how to do their job. The clearer your instructions, the better the results. Once everything is working as expected — launch it, and let your custom agent work for your users.

entry.title: From pipelines to products- when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.
entry.content:
What SAP actually changed: SAP becomes the producer

Data products are not new; the data-mesh community has argued for them since around 2019. What SAP did in Business Data Cloud (BDC) is structural: for SAP sources, SAP itself becomes the producer.

SAP-managed data products ship with the platform. They are pre-built extractions from S/4HANA and other SAP applications, landed and refreshed by SAP-managed pipelines into the BDC storage layer (HDLF), and published to the catalog with their business semantics attached. You do not build the extraction or its base semantics. You activate them, and build only the logic that is genuinely yours on top, which moves your scarce engineering effort from plumbing to differentiation. Where data-team bandwidth is the binding constraint, and for most organizations it is, that is the whole game.

This is not "only SAP data." There are three doors into the same governed catalog. Only the first is gated by an application purchase.

 

Why “zero-copy” is a fact, not a slogan

The foundation stores data products as open columnar files (Parquet, managed as Delta tables) in a managed object store built on SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF). File-based is the key phrase. A warehouse locks data inside a proprietary format, so the only way to share it is to export a copy. An object store keeps data as open files in a bucket, and open files can be read by many engines at once without anyone copying anything.

This is the lakehouse principle applied inside SAP: one physical copy in open Delta format, read by SAP Datasphere, SAP Databricks, and SAP HANA Cloud through a shared catalog. Inside the BDC boundary, that means produce once and consume across all three engines without duplicating the data. One copy, many readers, that is the mechanism the market calls "zero-copy."

The full pipeline: how data actually lands in HDLF

For technical audiences, here is the honest end-to-end. CDS views are the extraction interface throughout, but the pipeline runs in two contexts. In the SAP-managed context, SAP owns standard, SAP-released CDS views built on the One Domain Model, its cross-application semantic model that keeps core entities consistent. In the customer-managed context, you create or identify the CDS entity yourself: a standard view, an extended or Z-CDS view, a custom business object, or a table. The same physical machinery moves both, and that machinery is what the rest of this section walks through.

 

 

 

Extraction at a glance, by source system

 

Cloud S/4HANA activates SAP-managed data products directly; everything else, on-prem S/4HANA, ECC, and non-SAP, enters through the customer-managed door. The source differs, the entry point does not: CDS views over the Cloud Connector for on-prem, extractors and tables via a Datasphere connection for ECC, database and file connections for third-party. Whatever the origin, one Replication Flow performs the universal physical write into HDLF as Delta Parquet, landing in a single governed catalog.

 

One product, end to end, and back

Follow a single product from source to intelligence and back. It leaves S/4HANA through SAP-managed CDS extraction and lands in the BDC foundation as governed Delta files. It appears in the catalog carrying its schema, semantics, and lineage: the "app store" moment where you browse before you install. The customer activates it, making it consumable.

From there the same single product serves everything. Datasphere models it; SAP Analytics Cloud plans and reports on it; and when a data-science team wants it, it is shared to SAP Databricks over Delta Sharing, appearing in Unity Catalog as a mounted table while the bytes stay in the foundation. What crosses the share is the table and its column-level meaning: names, types, keys, and comments, so the data scientist sees labeled, described columns rather than naked ones. What does not cross is the multidimensional model or the source-side access controls, measures, hierarchies, and data-access rules live in Datasphere and SAC, not in the shared file, and are re-established on the consumer side. Zero-copy is real at the boundary; meaning travels at the column level, not the model level.

When the loop closes, the data product is published back into BDC as a new governed data product through a federated remote share, live and zero-copy, not a physical import.

One honest boundary

Good advisory work separates what a platform will show from what it will guarantee. So, plainly: Delta Sharing transports tables, not multidimensional models. Measures, hierarchies, and data-access controls do not survive the protocol and must be re-established on the consuming side. The semantics that carry over are the structural ones (keys, column names, comments, data types), not the full analytic model. Say this up front and you will be trusted; let people discover it later and you will not.

Go Deeper On Delta Sharing: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-managed-data-products-in-sap-bdc-what-really-happens-when-your/ba-p/14394675

The economics: what actually burns capacity

Most confusion about BDC cost comes from collapsing three things that are actually separate. Hold them apart and the economics stop being mysterious. This is the distinction solution architects and commercial teams have to be able to explain to a customer.

Illustrative split from a small worked example. Exact mix depends on data volume, refresh frequency, and workloads run.

The single most misunderstood point in the whole model: intelligent content does not consume capacity units. You license the application, but what burns capacity is the data products and infrastructure underneath it. Switching on more applications barely moves the meter. Cost follows the workloads you actually run on your data (provisioning, refresh, queries, and any optional ML in Databricks), not the number of applications you own.

Why this matters now

The reason the industry is talking about governed data products in 2026 is not nostalgia for data-mesh theory. It is AI. Agents and copilots fail in production mostly because they lack business context: they do not know that this table means settlement, or that this field is a posted invoice. A governed data product is that context, delivered with the data. The same foundation that ends your dashboard disagreements is what makes your S/4HANA data trustworthy enough to hand to an AI.

So the shift from pipelines to products is really two shifts at once. It retires a decade of brittle, unowned extractions and their hidden maintenance cost. And it builds the governed, meaningful foundation the next wave of enterprise AI actually requires, data that is AI-ready because it is governed and meaningful, not merely moved faster.

That is what changes when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.

SAP Enterprise Architecture Designer SAP Datasphere SAP Databricks SAP Analytics Cloud, connectivity SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: CMI's Playbook for Joule Integration: From One Document to Agentic AI
entry.content:
Joule is now natively grounded in over 400 CMI competitive assets (CompeteGPT) and over 4,500 customer references through ReferenceGPT via the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. Integration to Joule involved a lot more than just the technical work - finding the right resources, connecting with the right people, weeks of waiting when things broke and we didn't know what caused it or how to fix it, a few "aha" moments when we finally did, lots of testing and re-testing, and learning the process as we went.

One big takeaway at the end? If we knew what we know now, we could have shipped this in 3 months instead of 5.

With SAP's direction toward the autonomous suite, we wanted to share our learnings and exchange best practices as we head into our next phase: bringing an autonomous CMI Agent to life. This super-agent orchestrates knowledge gathering across all our internal CMI AI sub-agents to answer the most complex questions, and we can leverage shared common knowledge across the suite.

  • If your team is looking to add your documents or AI chatbot into Joule, this blog walks you through the starting steps, key contacts, links to essential resources, AI best practices, and lessons learned along the way. When we started, we were one of the very first teams doing this and had no peer insights to lean on.
  • If you're already ahead of the curve or interested in integrating our CMI agent into your system, we'd love to connect and learn from you. Especially as we move toward building a CMI agent that brings our full account intelligence to outside systems via A2A and MCP.

A little context first

If you're in sales and marketing, you've probably worked with CMI - the team that gathers and delivers competitive, customer and market intelligence inside SAP. For decades, CMI has served as SAP's market intelligence hub, collecting real-time signals from competitors, customers, industry analysts and translating them into actionable account insights, battlecards and alerts for our sales teams to win deals

But here's the core challenge we've been wrestling with: How do we collect and deliver intelligence in the AI era to the right person, at the right moment, while the information is still hot?

Imagine this: a single place where your team could find all the inter-connected intelligence from multiple channels they need using natural language. No more digging through scattered channels to get the right information. No more bookmarking standalone AI solutions on your laptop. Something you can access on your mobile with one prompt!

Joule became our answer - a way to scale this capability and ensure the right insights reach the right people at exactly the right time. A user inside Joule can now ask questions on how to win against a competitor, find a relevant customer reference, or uncover a market signal and get an intelligent, up-to-date answer powered by CMI intelligence. Without ever leaving their workflow. Anywhere, anytime.

So - how did we do it?

Stage 1: One Document, One Entry Point

We began with a single "CMI FAQ" document with resource links and key contacts, ingested into Joule. It provided guidance on where to find competitive intelligence, account insights, market models, analyst relations, and customer references.

For about a month, it worked fine. As thousands of documents flooded into Joule, our single CMI document became a needle in an ever-growing haystack. It didn’t show up in top search results for a variety of user prompts as it had limited information.

If you're just starting out and have limited documents/pdfs, you can Add your documents to Joule as a self-service with no coding required.

AI Tips for Better Results:

  • Write documents in an AI-friendly format for better Joule search results
  • Include prompt instructions and sample prompts within your document
  • Repeat key terms so your content ranks higher in search results
  • Expand all abbreviations (use hyperlinks where helpful)

Screenshots: before and after one document ingestion to Joule                                  

 

Stage 2: Grounding 400+ Competitive intelligence assets to Joule

The next natural question was obvious: if we could ingest one document, why not ingest all of them - every PDF, every doc, directly into Joule? This required a more sustainable solution without adding documents manually to the Self-service tool,

For weeks, we discussed the pros and cons of two technically complex options. Then one day, the Joule team presented us with a third path we hadn't considered.

Here were our choices:

Option A: Migrate our content from our database to SharePoint or AWS S3 so could Joule use it for document grounding?

Option B: Chunk the documents ourselves and share embeddings in SAP HANA DB with Joule?

Option C: Could Joule build an automated data pipeline, store it internally via API, and ground it automatically?

We went with Option C. Together with the Joule team; we built a direct data pipeline to Joule from our CMI CompeteOne database using API integration. All our competitive intelligence battlecards, analyst reports, CMI alerts are ingested in Joule every 24 hours.

Ready to get started? Check out the Fast Track Document Grounded Use Case Intake Guide to begin your integration.

Key Lessons: 

  • Talk to the Joule team early. Discuss your use-case upfront and partner with their technical team from day one. It saves months of rework. Also, dig into internal success stories on the One Joule SharePoint - you'll learn what works.
  • Prioritize business user testing. Get real users involved early to validate that Joule returns the right answers for real-world questions, this could inform how you write your documents to be AI-searchable.
  • Know the tracking gaps. Currently there is no usage of KPI tracking for grounded documents on the Joule dashboard.

Stage 3: Agentic Integration of our custom AI ReferenceGPT into Joule

The most ambitious part of our journey came next: bringing ReferenceGPT - our AI custom chatbot for finding the right SAP reference customers and their success stories into Joule as an agent via the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. This is where things got truly intelligent.

Instead of Joule simply retrieving a document, it could now hand off a request to ReferenceGPT- a specialized agent built specifically for this job and bring the answer back into the conversation. Two agents, working together.

Why A2A instead of native ingestion?

ReferenceGPT isn't just another set of documents. It's a specialized code-based agent that interacts with multiple data channels - customer success stories on digital library, customer references profiles stored on refHub on MXP platform as structured & nested tables, and account attributes from CRM. It has an in-built custom code logic to translate text to SQL to pull the most relevant references stored in tables in real time.

Example: User asks in Joule: "Find me a customer reference in the financial services industry who successfully replaced Oracle." Joule hands off to ReferenceGPT. ReferenceGPT finds the match, surfaces the reference profile, and returns the reference with contact details - all within seconds.

Navigating Technical Challenges

  • Capability deployment: Building this wasn't a one-click setup. It required navigating custom capability deployment to Joule process (DEV → TEST → PREPROD → PROD), PR approvals, and getting principal propagation set up across each environment. If you are building your code-base agents from scratch by leveraging the AI Golden Factory Path framework, it’s easier to have an A2A connection. In our case, we already had our own existing backend
  • Scenario activation: Joule uses an LLM to decide whether to hand off to ReferenceGPT. Initially, all activation tests were failing - Joule kept choosing to answer directly instead of routing to our agent. The fix? The scenario description needs to closely mirror how users naturally phrase their questions.

We rewrote ours to: "Identify and retrieve qualified SAP reference customers for sales opportunities. Search customers by fit criteria (industry, company size, region, technology), retrieve proof points (customer quotes, success stories, case studies), and connect with field contacts for customer reference validation." 

Result: 16 of 20 activation tests now pass consistently. Refer to the help Guide to test if your scenario is activated in Joule

  • Multi-turn conversation ("Show more"): Initially our follow-up prompt to the agent (e.g. "Show more") was treated as a brand-new query and didn’t carry forward the previous prompt context to ReferenceGPT. Since we show 5 references at a time, users typing "show more" weren't getting relevant follow-up results. Fix? Joule allows the capability to store conversational memory on your end using contextId and taskId from Joule multi-turn API
  • Joule’s UI card integration: You can display Joule response in Joule UI cards or simply text format. We decided against it because it's too inflexible for our use case but worth exploring as it may be useful in other scenarios.
  • Model choice matters: We tested both Claude Sonnet and GPT-4o as the LLM powering ReferenceGPT inside Joule. Claude Sonnet produced better quality but added latency (still within Joule's 60-second limit). Worth testing for your own use case.

Ready to start? See Joule A2A help guides

  • Code-based Agent Integration (Bring your own agent)
  • Bring Your Own Agents to Joule via A2A
  • ReferenceGPT A2A GitHub repo (as example)

Tips

  • Raise Joule Standard team issues via the internal ITSM ticket (CA-JOULE-CR) and refer to the Joule troubleshooting guide.
  • For principal propagation issues or to establish trust and do attribute mapping, get support from the Platform CoE team by creating tickets here.
  • Joule usage tracking: Usage can be viewed at the Joule Usage Dashboard. Filtering specific document collection is not currently supported. However, for an A2A agent scenario, it can be tracked by filtering the scenario name.

Where we go from here

We're continuing to expand CMI's presence inside Joule with CMI agents and MCPs - adding more intelligence sources, improving response quality, and exploring new ways to bring the right insight to the right person at the right moment. A2A isn't just about integration - it's about letting each tool do what it was designed to do, then orchestrating them to create something greater than the sum of its parts

Special thanks to the Joule team ( Kristiana Dimova, Rumyana Vaseva, Dragomir Drashkov, Alex Polevoy), CMI leadership ( Franklin Herbas, Paul Logue) , CPIT ( Damien Garnier, Christoph Kommer) and Global References team (John Gruber, Paul Smith, Kevin Scanlon)

entry.title: Document Grounding | Add context to Joule Q&A using information retrieved from custom documents
entry.content:
Document Grounding empowers SAP customers to get accurate, trustworthy answers from Joule by securely grounding conversations in their own business documents. Instead of relying solely on generic language model knowledge, Joule uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to pull relevant information from unstructured and semi-structured documents stored in SAP and approved third-party repositories.

This ensures responses are context-aware, factually correct, and based on the most up-to-date business data.

By tracing answers back to their source documents, Document Grounding increases transparency and accountability while enabling more reliable, use-case-specific insights directly within conversational workflows.

 
Benefits
  • Improve user self-reliance and user satisfaction
  • Improve the reliability and applicability of generated content
  • Reduce hallucinations or incorrect generated information

Set Up Document Grounding

The complete setup guide is available here Setup document grounding

 

Related Information
Metering and Pricing
Supported Regions

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Build: Q1 & Q2 2026 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Greetings, Builders! We blinked and just like that, the first half of 2026 is complete. There’s been a whirlwind of updates, including our recent announcement at SAPPHIRE of the new Joule Studio for intent-based development and agent creation.

Q1 and Q2 also brought a wave of innovations across SAP Build, helping our users extend and automate applications faster than ever. From AI-powered capabilities in SAP Build Work Zone, Joule Work Mobile, CAP, and SAPUI5, to improved visibility in process automation and more, these enhancements address some of your key pain points around developer productivity, simplified workflows, and user experience. Here are the Q1 and Q2 2026 release highlights from SAP Build, plus a look ahead at the new Joule Studio and how you can start learning the fundamentals of AI Agent building today.

Improved Customization and Gen AI in SAP Build Work Zone

No, it's not a social networking site from 2003, it's personalized, relevant SAP content.

Easy access to personalized content with My Space

Following the clear feedback from customers, it’s now possible to organize and manage your favorite Work Zone content on a dedicated, personal page. Many business users often have multiple roles, which may include content spanned across various spaces and structures. The “My Space” capability lets you bring all of that together on one page, allowing you to quickly access your relevant content and easily edit it to fit your specific needs.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ThomasHensel

Enhanced text generation and refinement with AI

The text widget toolbar now includes an AI option to generate text for the widget. In addition, AI can refine the text in input fields, text areas, and text widgets. This includes changing the tone of the text, adjusting its length, and fixing spelling and grammar.

Create whole workspaces, locate relevant content and more with Joule

At the end of last year, Joule became integrated into SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, bringing the AI assistant’s conversational intelligence right to your workspace. Now, Joule supports three new, frequently used scenarios:

  • Transactional: Create workspaces based on templates, upload content, or invite users to become members.
  • Navigational: Search for specific workspaces that you manage or are related to keywords.
  • Informational: Search for a specific user and show their profile.

You can trigger each of these scenarios through the familiar conversational experience with Joule, executing more tasks and finding key information faster.

👉 See example prompts and learn more on the Help Portal.

👉 See all the latest releases from SAP Build Work Zone.

Updates in Mobile: Seamless SSO, end-user support, MDK 26.6 & more

SAP Mobile Start’s rebranding, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on

SAP Mobile Start has been the established native mobile entry point for your company’s systems and daily tasks, including content from SAP Build Work Zone. The app has now been rebranded as the Joule Work mobile app. While the core functionalities remain the same as before, the new app includes an updated Joule icon, full-screen interface, and voice dictation of prompts. 

With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app also introduces a streamlined single sign-on for opening web applications in the in-app browser. For organizations with large mobile workforces, this eliminates the need for repetitive credential entry and creates a seamless workflow across applications. Learn more about these updates in the blog posts by @ThiloBerndt and @thomas-bruckner.

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality
  • Joule Work mobile app version 3.1 is out
  • Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app

Customizing in-app support for end users in Joule Work

Getting technical support for mobile app issues is a common frustration that often causes users to turn to the wrong channels, such as app store reviews or generic feedback forms. It’s now possible for companies to add support information, such as contact details, ticketing systems and resources, directly to your Joule Work mobile app and configure it in SAP Mobile Services. This enhancement transforms how your end users can access help when they need it most.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ivan_chaushev.

AI feedback forms, extended compliance checks and more in MDK Client 26.6

The latest release of Mobile development kit (MDK) is now available, bringing several significant upgrades to security, compliance and embedded AI in native mobile app development. Device compliance checks have now been extended to before a user onboards, allowing for earlier detection of compromised devices and stronger security. Enhancements to AI are also ongoing, including a new control that enables quick user feedback on AI-generated content.

👉 See the complete list of new MDK features in this blog post by @Jitendra_Kansal.

New CAP plugins for AI recommendations & improved traceability

RPT-1 generated recommendations with CAP AI plugin for node.js 

In pro-code development, SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) continues to provide productivity gains by allowing developers to reduce boilerplate code and focus on problem solving. The new SAP CAP AI plugin for node.js provides recommendations powered by RPT-1 within the in-app UI. The plugin supports multi-tenancy, simplifies AI Core configuration, and inherently embeds the enterprise readiness of CAP out-of-the-box.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

New and improved change tracking plugin

Version 2.0 of the change tracking plugin for CAP has been released and brings major improvements to how enterprise applications capture and visualize data changes. The new version includes tree table visualization, support for CDS Expression Language (CXL), and a fundamentally redesigned architecture. These changes bring significant upgrades to performance and traceability, allowing developers to exercise more control over changes and customizations.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

Streamlined SAPUI5 development with AI and developer extensibility

UI5 plugins for modernization and coding agents in GitHub Copilot

Keeping your SAPUI5 app up to date just got easier. The new UI5 Modernization Plugin automates the most time-consuming parts of app modernization: replacing deprecated APIs, eliminating global namespaces, and much more. The plugin is available for Claude Code as well as GitHub Copilot. Also now available for GitHub Copilot are UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents (previously known as UI5 Plugins for Claude). These bring up-to-date SAPUI5 knowledge directly into your AI-assisted development workflow.

The latest version of the UI5 MCP Server is also now available and supports the development of UI Integration Cards. It includes three new tools to assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Learn more about these plugins:

  • Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin: Modernize Your OpenUI5/SAPUI5 App in Hours, Not Weeks
  • UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents: Now Supporting GitHub Copilot
  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation

Key User Adaptation & Transitions for UI5 TypeScript developers

With key user adaptation, business users can change the UI of SAP Fiori standard apps directly in the runtime app. If more complex changes are needed, there now is a seamless transition from key user to developer extensibility in SAP Build.

TypeScript is going through its biggest transition in years. If you build SAPUI5 applications with TypeScript, we have provided a guide for developers around these changes.

👉 Learn more about key user adaptation and the TypeScript transition.

Extended visibility and proactive monitoring in process automation

Avoid manual error-tracing with automated exception handling.

Enhanced process visibility with instance-level attributes

Operational teams often lack business context in process monitoring. For a business user, this can make it difficult to see key details like approaching deadlines or decisions taken by the previous approver. Now with instance-level attributes enabled, you can add meaningful business context to process instances, allowing users to easily search, filter, group, and analyze processes. This allows teams to improve their decision-making and reduce the risk of breaching SLAs.

👉 Learn more about instance-level attributes in the Help Portal.

Process group connections & other updates

More updates are now available in process automation, focused on improved visibility, proactive monitoring, and enhanced governance. Here are some highlights:

  • Process group connections (1-to-many): Organizations often need visibility across related processes, not just individual workflows. This enables end-to-end monitoring by linking a primary process with its associated downstream processes, providing a more complete view of business execution.
  • Notifications in Visibility Scenarios: Proactive email alerts notify users about processing failures, skipped instances, or event thresholds before they impact operations. This reduces manual monitoring and helps teams respond to issues faster.
  • Environment Filters: New controls allow users to view or restrict process visibility by environment. This improves governance and makes it easier to manage and analyze process data across development, test, and production landscapes.
  • Event handler to catch Action errors: Introduces configurable Retry mechanisms and structured exception handling at the Action level. This allows organizations to systematically manage errors, while reducing manual and time-consuming troubleshooting efforts.

👉 See all the latest releases and documentation on the Help Portal.

Looking ahead to the new Joule Studio

If you're looking to learn more about the new Joule Studio and how you can use it to start creating AI agents, we have great news: the learning journey Introducing Joule Studio is now available! With this free course, you'll be able to understand the bigger picture and how Joule Studio fits into SAP Business AI Platform. You'll also get to know what you can do with Joule Studio and Joule Work, how you can use it to create agents and automate workflows with n8n.

👉 Start learning today: Introducing Joule Studio

entry.title: Discover the 18 AI-Assisted Procedures in the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series that introduces AI in SAP Activate. For more information, visit the main blog "SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation."

With the 2608 release, the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private – New Implementation roadmap now includes 18 AI-assisted procedures designed to help implementation teams accelerate common project activities.

These procedures are available throughout the implementation lifecycle, supporting activities in the Prepare, Explore, Realize, and Deploy phases.

📷 Image 1 – AI-assisted procedures distributed across the roadmapFinding AI-Assisted Procedures

The easiest way to locate AI-assisted procedures in the roadmap is by using the AI-Assisted Procedure tag to browse all AI-enabled tasks.

📷 Image 2 – Searching for AI-Assisted Procedure in the Road Map Viewer

What to Expect

Each AI-assisted procedure provides guidance for a specific implementation activity. Depending on the task, you'll find:

  • Recommended prompts
  • Instructions for using SAP Joule for Consultants
  • Guidance for generating implementation artifacts
  • Tips for reviewing and refining AI-generated content

The goal is not to replace consultant expertise, but to help teams get started more quickly and consistently.

📷 Image 3 – Example of an AI-assisted procedure

Prompt Library

Many AI-assisted procedures include a Prompt Library with ready-to-use prompts that can be copied, adapted to your project context, and executed using SAP Joule for Consultants.

These prompts provide a solid starting point and help reduce the effort required to create common project deliverables.

📷 Image 4 – Prompt Library within an AI-assisted procedure

Start Exploring

The addition of 18 AI-assisted procedures marks another step in bringing AI directly into the SAP Activate methodology. Whether you're planning workshops, documenting requirements, or preparing for deployment, these procedures help you identify where AI can accelerate your work while keeping SAP Activate at the center of your implementation approach.

Ready to explore them? Open the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private – New Implementation roadmap in the SAP Road Map Viewer and search for "AI-Assisted Procedure" to discover all available AI-enabled tasks.
